title: Financial Assistance Application
description: Check if you qualify for charity care or financial assistance at a nonprofit hospital

what_is_charity_care.title: Most hospitals must offer financial help, and most patients never ask
what_is_charity_care.body:
The majority of hospitals in the United States are nonprofit. In exchange for their tax-exempt status, federal law requires them to maintain a Financial Assistance Policy (FAP) and help patients who qualify.

This isn't charity in the traditional sense. It's a legal obligation. Depending on your income and the hospital's policy, your bill could be reduced by 25%, 50%, 75%, or even eliminated entirely.

Many hospitals offer free care to families earning up to 200% of the Federal Poverty Level, and reduced-cost care up to 400%.

callout.text: Under Section 501(r) of the Internal Revenue Code, nonprofit hospitals must have a Financial Assistance Policy and cannot use extraordinary collection actions (lawsuits, liens, garnishment) without first screening patients for eligibility.

hospital_type.prompt: Is the hospital that treated you a nonprofit?
hospital_type.subtext: Most hospitals in the US are nonprofit, including most major health systems. If you're not sure, that's okay. We'll help you figure it out.

options.label: I'm not sure
options.helpText: Most hospitals are nonprofit. If the hospital's name doesn't include words like 'Community,' 'Memorial,' or 'University,' you can check on the IRS Tax Exempt Organization Search.

for_profit_options.title: For-profit hospitals aren't required to offer charity care, but they often will negotiate
for_profit_options.body:
For-profit hospitals aren't bound by Section 501(r), so they don't have a legal obligation to offer financial assistance. However, many for-profit hospitals still have hardship programs because collecting some payment is better than none.

Here's what you can do:

• Call their billing department and ask about payment plans or hardship discounts
• Request an itemized bill to check for errors first
• Ask if they offer a prompt-pay discount (many offer 20-40% off for upfront payment)
• Negotiate. Hospitals regularly accept less than the billed amount.

callout.text: Even without a legal right to financial assistance, many patients successfully negotiate their bills down 30-60% simply by asking.

how_to_check.title: How to check if your hospital is nonprofit
how_to_check.body:
There are a few quick ways to find out:

1. Search the IRS Tax Exempt Organization database at apps.irs.gov/app/eos
2. Look at the hospital's website. Nonprofit hospitals usually mention their tax-exempt status in their 'About' section
3. Call the hospital's billing department and ask directly: 'Is this hospital a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization?'
4. Check your state's hospital association website

If the hospital is part of a large health system (like HCA, Ascension, CommonSpirit, etc.), a quick web search for '[system name] nonprofit status' will usually tell you.

Don't worry if you can't confirm. You can still send the application. The worst they can say is no.

callout.text: About 57% of US hospitals are nonprofit. If yours is one of them, they are legally required to have a Financial Assistance Policy.

collect_hospital_info.subtext: Enter the name and address from the bill you received.

validation.message: Please enter a valid ZIP code (e.g., 12345 or 12345-6789)

collect_bill_info.subtext: This information will be included in your application so the hospital can locate your account.

fields.label: Account or reference number
fields.placeholder: From the top of your bill

collect_financial_info.subtext: This is used to determine your eligibility for financial assistance. Be honest, because the hospital may ask for documentation.

meta.helpText: Your income and household information stays in your browser. We never see or store this data.

fields.label: Your total annual household income (before taxes)
Number of people in your household

validation.message: Please enter a number between 1 and 20

collect_sender_info.subtext: This is who the letter is from. Your name and address will appear on the application.

meta.helpText: We need your name and address so the hospital can identify your account and respond to you. This information stays in your browser.

fields.label: Your full name
fields.placeholder: As it appears on your medical records

validation.message: Please enter a valid ZIP code

likely_eligible.title: You likely qualify for significant financial assistance
likely_eligible.body:
Based on the information you provided, your household income appears to be at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level. Most nonprofit hospitals offer free or near-free care at this income level.

This is a strong application. The letter we're about to generate will formally request a review of your eligibility under the hospital's Financial Assistance Policy.

callout.text: Many hospitals will forgive the entire bill for patients at your income level. Don't settle for a payment plan without first applying for financial assistance.

possibly_eligible.title: You may qualify for reduced-cost care
possibly_eligible.body:
Your household income appears to be between 200% and 400% of the Federal Poverty Level. Many nonprofit hospitals offer sliding-scale discounts at this level, typically 25% to 75% off the bill.

Every hospital sets its own thresholds, so it's always worth applying. The letter we generate will request a formal review of your eligibility.

callout.text: Even if the hospital's standard policy doesn't cover your income level, many hospitals grant exceptions on a case-by-case basis. Always apply.

higher_income_options.title: Your income is above typical thresholds, but you should still apply
higher_income_options.body:
Your household income appears to be above 400% of the Federal Poverty Level, which is above the threshold most hospitals use. However:

• Some hospitals have higher thresholds (up to 500% or even 600% FPL)
• Hospitals may consider extraordinary medical expenses, job loss, or other hardships
• You may qualify for an interest-free payment plan
• The hospital may offer a self-pay discount regardless of income

There is no downside to applying. The worst they can say is no.

callout.text: Include any extenuating circumstances in your application: recent job loss, other medical bills, disability, etc. Hospitals have discretion to grant assistance beyond their standard thresholds.

nextSteps.text: Print this letter and send it via certified mail with return receipt requested
nextSteps.details: Certified mail creates a legal record that the hospital received your application. Keep the receipt.

nextSteps.text: Also submit the hospital's own financial assistance application form
nextSteps.details: Most hospitals have their own form in addition to accepting letters. Call the billing department and ask for their Financial Assistance Application. This letter covers you while you complete their process.
The hospital will likely ask for: recent tax return, pay stubs (2-3 months), bank statements, proof of other expenses (rent, other medical bills). Having these ready speeds up the process.

nextSteps.text: Request that collection activity be paused while your application is reviewed
nextSteps.details: Under 501(r), nonprofit hospitals cannot take extraordinary collection actions while a financial assistance application is pending. Your letter includes this request.

nextSteps.text: Follow up if you don't hear back within 30 days
nextSteps.details: Call the billing department and reference your certified mail receipt date. Ask for the status of your financial assistance application.

nextSteps.text: If denied, appeal and consider requesting an itemized bill
nextSteps.details: You can appeal a denial. Also request an itemized bill. If there are billing errors, getting those corrected may reduce the bill enough to bring it within your means.
